title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Information
meta_description: Discover why your gut feeling is a powerful tool for evaluating information. Learn to trust your intuition and turn sensitivity into a superpower.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Information
og_description: Discover why your gut feeling is a powerful tool for evaluating information. Learn to trust your intuition and turn sensitivity into a superpower.
body:
You've Been Told You're "Too Sensitive" - Here's Why That's Your Superpower

You know that feeling. You're reading an article, scrolling through a social media post, or listening to someone explain something. And something in your gut says, "Nope. That's not right." But then you look at the facts, the data, the logical argument. And it all checks out. So you ignore that feeling. You push it down. You tell yourself you're being irrational, paranoid, or just plain wrong.

But the feeling doesn't go away. It sits there, a low hum of unease in your chest. And later, you find out the source was biased, the data was cherry-picked, or the person was lying. And you kick yourself. "I knew it," you mutter. "I knew something was off."

That's your intuition. And it's not wrong. It's just been drowned out by a world that worships logic and dismisses feeling. You're here because you're ready to stop second-guessing that inner knowing. You're ready to use it as the sharp tool it actually is. Let's get into how.

Why Your Gut Is Smarter Than Your Brain (Sometimes)

Here's the thing most people don't get. Your intuition isn't some mystical, woo-woo magic. It's your brain processing information faster than your conscious mind can keep up. Your nervous system picks up on micro-expressions, tone of voice, pacing, word choice, and even the energy of a room. It cross-references all of that with your past experiences, your stored memories, and your learned patterns. And then it sends you a signal. A feeling. A knowing.

Your logical brain, on the other hand, works slowly. It needs time to analyze, compare, and conclude. It's great at math and spreadsheets. It's terrible at reading people and detecting bullshit. Right?!

So when you're evaluating information, you have two systems running simultaneously. One is fast, pattern-based, and feels like a hunch. The other is slow, analytical, and feels like thinking. Most of us were trained to only trust the second one. But that's like trying to drive a car with one eye closed. You're missing half the road.

"Your intuition is not the enemy of logic. It's the scout who goes ahead and reports back before the army arrives. Trust the scout."

The First Signal: Physical Sensations

Your body never lies. It can't. Your nervous system is wired for survival. It's been calibrated over millions of years to detect threats and opportunities. And it communicates with you through physical sensations. Tightness in your chest. A knot in your stomach. A sudden chill. A wave of heat. A feeling of expansion or contraction.

When you're evaluating information, start by checking in with your body. Before you even read the full article or listen to the full explanation, pause. Scan your body from head to toe. What do you feel? Where do you feel it?

If you feel a tightening or a closing down, that's a red flag. Something in the information is triggering a protective response. It might be a lie, a manipulation, or simply something that doesn't align with your truth. If you feel a sense of expansion, lightness, or warmth, that's a green light. Your system is saying, "This feels right. This is safe."

Does that land? Because this is the foundation. Your body is your first and most reliable source of intuitive data. Ignore it at your own risk.

If you want to go deeper into this body-based intuition, I highly recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not a spiritual book. It's a neuroscience book that explains exactly why your body holds the truth. See on Amazon (paid link)

Second Signal: Emotional Resonance

Your emotions are not your enemy. They're not irrational distractions. They're data. Pure, unfiltered data about your environment. When you're evaluating information, notice what emotions come up. Do you feel a sense of calm? Excitement? Curiosity? Or do you feel defensive, angry, anxious, or numb?

Here's the trick. The emotion itself isn't the problem. It's what it tells you. If the information makes you feel defensive, ask yourself: "Am I defending something I believe? Or is this information threatening a comfortable lie?" If it makes you feel anxious, ask: "Is this anxiety about the content itself, or about what I might have to change if I accept it?"

Your emotions are like a compass. They point toward truth, but only if you're willing to follow the needle. Most people get stuck in the emotion itself. They feel angry and then act from that anger. Instead, use the emotion as a signal. Let it tell you where to look, not what to think.

For example, let's say you're reading a news article that makes you feel furious. Your first instinct might be to share it, comment on it, or argue with it. But if you pause, you can ask: "Why am I so angry? Is the article designed to make me angry? Is it using emotional triggers to bypass my critical thinking?" That pause is where your intuition meets your logic. And that's where real discernment happens.

Third Signal: Timing and Synchronicity

Intuition doesn't always come as a feeling. Sometimes it comes as a timing. You stumble across information at the exact moment you need it. Or you hear the same idea from three different sources in one week. Or you have a sudden urge to check a source, and you find it's been debunked.

This is your intuition working through your environment. It's not random. It's your subconscious mind guiding you toward or away from certain information. Pay attention to these coincidences. They're not coincidences. They're breadcrumbs.

When you're evaluating information, ask yourself: "How did this come to me? Did I seek it out? Or did it appear? Does it feel like it's meant for me, or does it feel forced?" Trust the timing. If something feels off about how the information arrived, that's a signal.

I know it sounds a little out there. But think about it. How many times have you been thinking about a specific topic, and then an article or a video about it shows up in your feed? That's not just an algorithm. That's your intuition, working through your attention, pulling you toward what you need to see.

If you want to strengthen this skill, check out The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It's all about how your intuition picks up on threats and opportunities before your conscious mind does. It's a practical guide to listening to those signals. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Five-Step Intuition Check for Any Information

Let's make this practical. Here's a five-step process you can use right now, the next time you're reading something that feels off or too good to be true.

Step 1: Pause and Breathe

Before you react, take three slow breaths. This calms your nervous system and brings you into your body. You can't access your intuition when you're in fight-or-flight. You need to be present.

Step 2: Scan Your Body

Close your eyes for a second. Scan from your head to your feet. Where do you feel tension? Where do you feel ease? Notice any sensations without judging them. Just observe.

Step 3: Name the Emotion

What emotion is present? Is it fear? Excitement? Curiosity? Anger? Sadness? Don't try to change it. Just name it. "I am feeling anxious right now." That act of naming creates distance between you and the emotion. It gives you space to choose your response.

Step 4: Ask the Key Question

Here's the question that changes everything: "Is this information serving my highest good, or is it trying to manipulate me?" This cuts through the noise. If the information is designed to make you afraid, angry, or desperate, it's probably manipulation. If it leaves you feeling informed, empowered, or curious, it's probably aligned with your truth.

Step 5: Check the Source

Now use your logic. Who wrote this? What's their agenda? Are they selling something? Are they trying to change your behavior? Do they have a history of accuracy? Your intuition can flag the issue, but your logic needs to verify it. Use both.

When Your Intuition and Logic Disagree

This is the hard part. Sometimes your gut says one thing, and the facts say another. What do you do?

First, don't panic. This is normal. It means you're fully human. Second, don't automatically side with logic. Logic is great, but it's also limited. It can only work with the information it has. Your intuition might be picking up on something your logic hasn't seen yet.

Here's a trick. Ask your intuition a direct question. "What am I missing?" Then sit quietly for a minute. See what comes up. It might be a memory, a word, a feeling, or an image. Don't force it. Just let it surface.

Then, ask your logic the same question. "What am I missing?" Look at the information again. Check the sources. Look for gaps. Compare both answers. Often, you'll find that your intuition was pointing to a missing piece that your logic couldn't see.

I've had this happen dozens of times. I'll read something that looks perfectly reasonable. All the facts check out. But my stomach is tight. I can't shake the feeling that something is wrong. So I dig deeper. And I find the hidden agenda. The cherry-picked data. The missing context. Every single time, my intuition was right. It just needed my logic to catch up.

How to Practice This Daily

You don't get good at this by reading one article. You get good by practicing. Every day. Here are three simple practices you can start today.

Practice 1: The Three-Second Check

Before you click on any article, video, or social media post, pause for three seconds. Ask yourself: "What do I expect to find here? What's my intention for reading this?" That tiny pause engages your intuition. You'll start to notice which content pulls you in and which content you're just reacting to.

Practice 2: The Journal Check

Keep a small notebook or a note on your phone. Every time you have a strong intuitive hit about something you read, write it down. Note the date, the source, and your feeling. Then, later, check back. Was your intuition right? This builds trust in your own system.

Practice 3: The Energy Check

This one is a little more advanced, but it's powerful. When you're reading something, imagine the energy of the words. Does it feel heavy or light? Does it feel expansive or constricting? Does it feel like it's coming from a place of love or fear? This is pure intuitive discernment. Trust what you feel.

What To Do When You're Overwhelmed

Let's be real. The world is drowning in information. News, opinions, ads, propaganda, memes, hot takes. It's exhausting. And when you're tired, your intuition gets fuzzy. You can't access it. You start reacting instead of responding.

If you're feeling overwhelmed, step back. Unplug. Go for a walk. Sit in silence. Do something with your hands. Your intuition is like a radio signal. It's always there, but if there's too much noise, you can't hear it. You need to turn down the volume on the outside world to hear the inside world.

This is not weakness. This is wisdom. Know what I mean? The most intuitive people I know are also the most protective of their quiet time. They don't consume information all day. They consume it in small, intentional doses. Then they process it. Then they integrate it.

If you're struggling with information overload, I recommend Digital Minimalism by Cal Newport. It's not about intuition directly, but it's about creating the space you need to hear your own inner voice. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Bottom Line (No Summary, Just Truth)

You were born with a built-in bullshit detector. It's been there since day one. But you were taught to distrust it. You were taught that feelings are unreliable, that logic is king, that you need proof for everything. And that's partly true. You do need proof. But you also need your gut. They're not enemies. They're partners.

The next time you're evaluating information, don't ignore that little voice. Don't override that feeling in your chest. Listen to it. Ask it questions. Let it guide your investigation. Then use your logic to verify. That's the dance. That's the art. And you can learn it.

Start small. Start today. Pick one piece of information you're unsure about. Run it through the five-step check. See what happens. You might be surprised at how accurate your intuition already is. You just needed permission to trust it.

And if you want to go deeper into this work, I've built a short assessment that will show you exactly where your intuitive strengths are and where you can grow. It takes about five minutes. And it might change how you see yourself.

Take the assessment here: intuitivesawaken.com/assessment
